items.q: In a zero-indexed array of 10 elements, what is the index of the last element?
items.correct: 9 - indices run from 0 to 9, so the last index is the length minus one.
items.misconception: 10 - the last element's index equals the array's length.
items.why: The classic off-by-one error. Counting starts at 0, so element number n lives at index n-1; forgetting this causes out-of-bounds errors in real code.

items.q: What data type is the value 7.0?
items.correct: Real (float) - the decimal point makes it a real number in most languages, even though its fractional part happens to be zero.
items.misconception: Integer - 7.0 is a whole number so it must be an integer.
items.random_wrong: String - anything containing a dot is text.
items.why: Students classify by mathematical value rather than representation. Type is determined by how the literal is written and stored: 7 is an integer, 7.0 is a real.

items.q: Which item does a stack remove first?
items.correct: The most recently added item - stacks are LIFO, with both push and pop operating at the top.
items.misconception: The first item that was added, because items leave in arrival order.
items.random_wrong: Whichever item is the largest value.
items.why: Queue behaviour gets transplanted onto stacks. Picture plates: you take the one most recently put on top, not the one at the bottom of the pile.

items.q: Which data type is most appropriate for storing a value that is either True or False?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Boolean. “Integer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An element of a two-dimensional array is identified by:
items.correct: Two indices, typically row and column
items.why: It's Two indices, typically row and column. “A single index” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which structure removes items in first-in-first-out order?
items.why: It's Queue. “Stack” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A record differs from an array because a record:
items.correct: Can group fields of different data types under one identifier
items.misconception: Must contain elements all of the same type
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Can group fields of different data types under one identifier. “Must contain elements all of the same type” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An array is declared with indices running from 0 to 9. How many elements does it contain?
items.why: The real figure is 10 elements. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
